blocks.text: Laura Phillips
Chief Financial Officer
Johnson, Cross and Gibbs
Dear Ms. Phillips:

This letter sets out the terms on which our firm will conduct a review of physician compensation arrangements for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}. It follows our discussion of the past several weeks and replaces the outline I left with you at that meeting. If the terms below match your understanding, please sign where indicated and return a copy to me. We are prepared to begin on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}}.

I want to be plain at the outset about what this engagement is and is not. You have asked whether the compensation arrangements you have in place are defensible on a fair-market-value basis. We will tell you what our analysis supports. Where the record is thin, we will say that the record is thin rather than fill the gap with an inference, and where a conclusion is qualified we will state the qualification in the same sentence as the conclusion. I mention this because engagement letters often promise comfort. We do not promise comfort. We promise work you can hand to counsel without apologizing for it.
Scope of Work

We will review the physician compensation arrangements you identify to us at kickoff, together with the underlying employment and professional services agreements, the productivity and quality data used to calculate compensation under those agreements, and the documentation supporting how each arrangement was approved. Our review will address whether the compensation payable under each arrangement is consistent with fair market value and commercially reasonable, and whether the arrangement as administered matches the arrangement as written. That last point is frequently where the difficulty sits.

Our work will not constitute a legal opinion, an audit, an examination, or a review conducted in accordance with attestation standards, and it should not be relied upon as any of those. We are not counsel and will not opine on the application of any statute or regulation to your arrangements. We will not perform procedures to detect fraud. Our conclusions will rest on the documents and data you provide and on the representations of the people we interview; we will not independently verify the completeness of what we are given, though we will tell you when something we expected to see is missing.
Approach

We work in three passes. First, we assemble the arrangement inventory and the documents behind it, and we reconcile the compensation actually paid against the compensation the agreements provide for. Second, we develop the fair-market-value analysis for each arrangement, using published survey data appropriate to the specialty, geography, and productivity profile, and we document which comparables we relied on and which we set aside and why. Third, we walk you and your counsel through findings before anything is final, so that factual corrections happen while corrections are still cheap.

You will receive a written report at the end. Findings will lead it. Recommendations, if we have any, will appear in a separate section and will be labeled as recommendations, so that no one reading the report later can quote a suggestion of ours as though it were a finding of ours.
The Team

Daniel Alvarado, Senior Consultant, will lead the engagement day to day and will own the fair-market-value analysis and the report. Daniel does this work constantly and is the person I send when counsel will be reading closely. Patricia Moore, Analyst, will handle the data reconciliation and the arrangement inventory, and will be your team's contact for document requests. I will serve as engagement partner. I read every deliverable before it leaves this office, and I am available to you directly at any point in the engagement, including on questions you would rather not put in writing to the team.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the scope described above is {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}}, inclusive of out-of-pocket costs other than travel, which we will bill at actual cost with your prior approval. We will invoice monthly in arrears based on progress, and invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. If the scope changes materially, whether because the arrangement inventory turns out larger than described or because you ask us to extend into an area not contemplated here, we will stop, tell you what the change means for fee and schedule, and proceed only on your written agreement. We do not accumulate out-of-scope time and present it to you afterward.
Term and Termination

The engagement begins on the start date noted above and continues until we deliver the final report or until terminated as provided here. Either of us may terminate on ten days' written notice to the other. If you terminate, you will owe fees for work performed through the termination date, and we will deliver our work product in whatever state it then stands, with a written note of what remained unfinished so that no one mistakes a draft for a conclusion. We may also withdraw if we determine that continuing would place us in a position inconsistent with our professional obligations, and we would tell you why.
Confidentiality

We will hold in confidence all non-public information you provide, will use it only for this engagement, and will restrict access to personnel who need it for the work. Where the information you provide includes protected health information, we will handle it consistent with your policies and applicable law, and we will ask you to limit what you send us to the minimum necessary for the analysis; we would rather receive de-identified productivity data than a file that carries patient identifiers we have no use for. We will not disclose your confidential information to any third party except with your consent or as compelled by law or regulatory authority, and in that event we will notify you promptly unless notification is itself prohibited. Our working papers remain our property. We will retain them for our standard retention period and then destroy them.
Limitation of Liability

Our aggregate liability to you arising out of or relating to this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under this letter. Neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect, incidental, consequential, special, or punitive damages, or for lost profits, whether or not the possibility of such damages was known. This limitation does not apply to liability arising from our fraud or willful misconduct. Any claim must be brought within one year after the date of the final deliverable to which it relates.

Our report is prepared for your internal use and for your counsel. It is not to be quoted, referred to, or distributed to any third party, in whole or in part, without our prior written consent, and we assume no duty to any third party who obtains it. If a regulator or other authority requests it, tell us and we will cooperate with you on the response.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict-of-laws provisions. If any provision of this letter is held unenforceable, the remainder stays in force. This letter, together with any written scope change signed by both of us, is the entire agreement between us on this subject and supersedes any prior discussion or proposal.
Acceptance

If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign below and return one copy to my attention. A signed copy is what starts the clock on our side, and Patricia Moore will send the initial document request the same day we receive it. Should anything here need adjustment before you sign, call me rather than marking up the letter; it is faster, and I would rather hear the objection than read it.
We appreciate the opportunity to work with Johnson, Cross and Gibbs on this review, and we take seriously that you are bringing us a question with real consequences attached to the answer.
Very truly yours,
Memorandum

items: To: Engagement team
From: Patricia Moore
Re: Kickoff, physician compensation review, Johnson, Cross and Gibbs

blocks.text: Objectives

The signed letter is back and the engagement opened on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}}. What {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} has asked us for is narrower than the conversation that produced it, so I want the objective written down before anyone starts pulling data. We are determining, for each compensation arrangement in the inventory the client gives us, whether the compensation payable is consistent with fair market value and commercially reasonable, and whether the arrangement as administered matches the arrangement as written. Everything else is out of scope until Laura says otherwise in writing.

The second half of that objective is the part I expect to occupy us. Agreements say one thing; the payroll file says what actually happened. Daniel Alvarado's view, and I agree with it, is that we should not form any opinion on the written arrangement until we can reconcile it to what was paid, because a clean agreement administered loosely is a different problem from a loose agreement, and the client needs to know which one they have. I would rather find the gap ourselves in week two than have counsel find it in month three.
Workstreams

items: Arrangement inventory. I own this. Build the complete list of physician compensation arrangements in scope, with the governing agreement and all amendments attached to each entry. The inventory is not done when the client's list is transcribed; it is done when every arrangement on the client's list ties to a document we hold and every physician on the payroll extract ties to an arrangement. Discrepancies in either direction go on the open-items log, not into a footnote.

Payment reconciliation. Also mine. For each arrangement, tie compensation actually paid over the review period back to the calculation the agreement specifies. Where I cannot tie it, I want the sequence documented step by step, from the productivity capture through to the payroll entry, so we can name the point where it diverges rather than asserting that it diverges.

Fair-market-value analysis. Daniel Alvarado owns this. Survey selection, comparable set, specialty and geography matching, and the written record of what was relied on and what was set aside. Nothing in this workstream starts until the inventory for a given arrangement is closed, because re-running an analysis against a corrected inventory wastes the analysis and the client's money.

Approval documentation. Shared. Collect the record of how each arrangement was approved and by whom. This is usually the thinnest file and the one that takes longest to shake loose, which is why it starts now and not later.

Open-items log. Mine to maintain. Every document we asked for and have not received, every question we asked and have not had answered, with the date asked. This goes to the client with each status update. It is not a complaint file; it is how we keep the schedule honest when a delay is on their side.

blocks.text: Working practices

Workpapers as you go, not at the end. Every figure that reaches a deliverable needs to be something one of us can produce on request within a minute, with its source named. If a draft asserts something we have not traced end to end, I will send it back, and I would rather do that on an internal draft than have Laura do it on a client-ready one.

On data: ask for the minimum. We agreed to limit what comes over to what the analysis needs, so productivity extracts should come to us de-identified at the physician-encounter level. If someone at the client offers us a fuller file because it is easier for them to pull, decline it politely and tell me.
Next steps

items: Initial document request went to Laura Phillips, Chief Financial Officer, the day the signed letter arrived. Response window is two weeks.
Kickoff call with the client's finance and legal contacts to be scheduled in the next several days. Daniel Alvarado leads; I will circulate an agenda beforehand and written notes after.
I will stand up the inventory template and the open-items log this week and send both around for comment before we populate anything.
Once the first tranche of agreements lands, we split the reading: I take the productivity and payment mechanics, Daniel Alvarado takes the compensation terms themselves.
Flag anything that looks like a scope question to me immediately. Do not absorb it.

blocks.text: Physician Compensation Review

items: Prepared for Laura Phillips, Chief Financial Officer, Johnson, Cross and Gibbs
Client: {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}
Engagement opened {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}}
Team: Daniel Alvarado, Senior Consultant; Patricia Moore, Analyst
Purpose of this briefing: confirm scope, report where the work stands, surface what we need from you

blocks.text: What We Were Asked To Do

items: Determine whether compensation payable under each in-scope arrangement is consistent with fair market value
Determine whether each arrangement is commercially reasonable
Determine whether arrangements as administered match arrangements as written
Not in scope: legal opinion, audit, attestation, or any conclusion on regulatory application

blocks.text: Where The Work Stands

items: Arrangement inventory built and reconciled against the payroll extract; a small number of entries remain open pending documents
Payment reconciliation underway for the arrangements whose inventory entries are closed
Fair-market-value analysis has begun on the closed entries only, by design
Approval documentation collection is open and is currently our thinnest file
No findings are being reported today. It is too early, and we will not preview a conclusion the analysis has not reached.

blocks.text: Method, Briefly

items: Fair market value is a term of art here, not an adjective, and Daniel Alvarado will document every comparable relied on and every one set aside
Survey selection matched to specialty, geography, and productivity profile
Patricia Moore ties every figure in a deliverable to a workpaper we can produce on request
Findings will lead the report; recommendations will appear separately and be labeled as such

blocks.text: What We Need From You

items: Remaining executed agreements and amendments for the open inventory entries
Approval records: committee minutes, memoranda, or whatever exists for each arrangement
Named contacts for two interviews on how productivity is captured and how it reaches payroll
Continued de-identified extracts only. Please do not send files carrying patient identifiers.
The open-items log accompanies this deck and carries the date each item was requested

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Close the remaining inventory entries as documents arrive
Complete payment reconciliation and identify any point where paid compensation diverges from the agreement's calculation
Extend the fair-market-value analysis across the full closed inventory
Working session with Johnson, Cross and Gibbs finance and legal to walk through the reconciliation approach
Next status update to follow on the agreed reporting cycle

blocks.text: Meeting Minutes: Working Session, Physician Compensation Review
Prepared by Daniel Alvarado. These minutes record what was discussed and agreed. Where a matter was raised and not resolved, it is recorded as raised and not resolved.
Attendees

items: Daniel Alvarado, Senior Consultant, Verdant Health Advisory (chair)
Patricia Moore, Analyst, Verdant Health Advisory
Laura Phillips, Chief Financial Officer, Johnson, Cross and Gibbs

blocks.text: Discussion

The session covered the state of the arrangement inventory and the reconciliation of paid compensation to the calculations the agreements provide for. Patricia Moore walked the group through the reconciliation as it currently stands. The inventory for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} is largely closed. A small number of entries remain open, in each case because an executed amendment has been identified in correspondence but not yet produced to us. Ms. Phillips undertook to locate them.

Patricia Moore reported one recurring pattern in the reconciliation and was careful to characterize it as a pattern rather than a finding. For several arrangements, compensation paid in a period does not tie to the productivity captured in that period, and the difference is not explained by the true-up mechanism the agreements describe. She has traced the sequence for two arrangements from encounter capture through to the payroll entry and can produce the workpapers on request. She has not yet traced it for the remainder and declined to generalize until she has. Her stated position was that a pattern in two arrangements is a reason to look at the other arrangements, not a conclusion about them.

Ms. Phillips asked whether the firm was in a position to say that the arrangements are defensible. The chair restated the scope. Our work addresses whether compensation payable under each arrangement is consistent with fair market value, whether the arrangement is commercially reasonable, and whether administration matches the written terms. Defensibility is a legal judgment and belongs to counsel. What the firm will supply is the factual and valuation record on which counsel forms that judgment. Ms. Phillips accepted the distinction and asked that counsel be included in the walkthrough of findings when the time comes. The chair agreed and noted that this is the firm's standard practice in any event.

On the fair-market-value analysis, the chair described the survey sources under consideration and the basis for matching comparables on specialty, geography, and productivity profile. Every comparable relied on will be documented, as will every comparable considered and set aside, with the reason. Ms. Phillips asked whether an arrangement at the upper end of a survey range is a problem. The chair declined to answer in the abstract. Position within a range is an input to the analysis and not a conclusion drawn from it, and the answer for any particular arrangement depends on the productivity and the documentation supporting it.

The approval documentation file was discussed and remains the weakest of the four workstreams. Ms. Phillips indicated that approvals for the older arrangements may exist only in committee minutes held by the corporate secretary. Patricia Moore noted that the open-items log records the original request date for each item and that several are now past the response window. No criticism was intended or, so far as the chair could tell, taken.
Action Items

rows: Produce executed amendments for the open inventory entries
Request committee minutes for older arrangements from the corporate secretary
Extend payment reconciliation to the remaining arrangements and document the sequence for each
Circulate the updated open-items log with request dates
Schedule two interviews on productivity capture and its path to payroll
Continue fair-market-value analysis on closed inventory entries and document comparable selection
Confirm counsel's participation in the findings walkthrough
Circulate these minutes for correction within five business days

blocks.text: Corrections to these minutes should be sent to the chair. Absent correction within the period noted above, they will stand as the record of the session.
Status Report: Physician Compensation Review

Prepared by Patricia Moore, Analyst. This report covers the period since the last update and is written for the client executive. It reports where the work stands, what it produced, what is next, and what could go wrong. It does not report findings; findings will come in the report, and none of what follows should be read as one.
Status Summary

The engagement is roughly at its midpoint and is on schedule. The arrangement inventory is closed except for entries awaiting executed amendments. Payment reconciliation is complete for the closed entries and in progress on the balance. The fair-market-value analysis is underway on the closed inventory and is proceeding at the pace we planned for. The approval documentation workstream is behind, and the reason is that we do not yet have the records; that is discussed under risks below.

Against the engagement budget of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}}, our fees incurred to date are consistent with the work completed and with the schedule. We are not carrying unbilled time and we have not performed work outside the scope of the letter. If anything arises that would change that, you will hear it from us before the work is done, not on an invoice afterward.
Accomplishments This Period

items: Closed the arrangement inventory for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} except for entries where an executed amendment is identified in correspondence but has not been produced to us. Every closed entry ties to a document we hold, and every physician on the payroll extract ties to an arrangement.

Completed the reconciliation of compensation paid to the calculation each agreement specifies, for all closed entries. Where the tie was not clean, the sequence is documented step by step from productivity capture through to the payroll entry.
Traced two arrangements end to end at the transaction level, which took longer than the others and was worth it: it gave us a documented sequence rather than an assertion.

Began the fair-market-value analysis on the closed inventory. Daniel Alvarado has the survey sources selected and the comparable-matching basis documented, including the comparables set aside and the reason for setting each aside.
Held a working session with your finance team on the reconciliation approach, and circulated minutes.
Maintained the open-items log, which accompanies this report and carries the date each item was first requested.

blocks.text: Upcoming Work

items: Extend the payment reconciliation across the remaining arrangements as the outstanding amendments arrive.
Complete the fair-market-value analysis across the full closed inventory.
Conduct two short interviews on how productivity is captured and how it reaches payroll. We have asked for named contacts and do not yet have them.
Assemble the approval documentation record for each arrangement.

Walk you and your counsel through findings before anything is final, so factual corrections happen while they are still cheap. Findings will lead the report; recommendations will be in their own section and labeled as recommendations.

blocks.text: Risks

Outstanding documents are the schedule risk, and at present they are the only one that concerns me. The fair-market-value analysis does not begin on an arrangement until that arrangement's inventory entry is closed, because re-running the analysis against a corrected inventory spends your fee twice. Every day an executed amendment sits unlocated is a day that arrangement's analysis has not started. The open-items log shows several requests now past the response window. This is not a complaint; it is the mechanism by which the schedule stays honest, and I would rather show it to you at the midpoint than explain it at the end.

The approval documentation file is the second risk and the one I would watch. If the records for the older arrangements exist only in committee minutes held elsewhere in your organization, retrieving them may take longer than the time remaining. If they do not exist, that is a fact about the record and we will report it as such, without characterizing what it means. Either way, we need to know which it is well before the report, and right now we do not.

A third item, noted for completeness rather than concern: the reconciliation has surfaced a pattern in the arrangements traced so far in which paid compensation does not tie to the productivity captured in the same period, and the difference is not explained by the true-up mechanism the agreements describe. Two arrangements are traced end to end. The rest are not. I am not prepared to say what the pattern means across the population until I have traced it, and neither Daniel Alvarado nor I will put it in a deliverable before then. It is in this report because you should not learn about it later, not because we have concluded anything.

Please direct questions on the reconciliation or the document requests to me, and questions on the valuation methodology to Daniel Alvarado. We appreciate the responsiveness of your team at Johnson, Cross and Gibbs, and the next update will follow on the agreed cycle.
